content.text: React uses reference equality to detect state changes. When you call
, React bails out and skips the re-render — even if you changed properties inside the object. The solution is to always produce a
object or array rather than modifying the existing one.

attrs.alt: Diagram: mutation keeps same reference so React skips re-render; immutable update produces new reference so React re-renders

content.text: ) copies all existing properties into a brand-new object literal. The new object has a different reference in memory, so React's
comparison detects the change and schedules a re-render.
— it copies only the top level. For nested objects, you must spread each level you are modifying.
If nested spreads become deeply verbose, consider flattening the state into separate
Arrays in JavaScript have mutating methods (
) and non-mutating ones. Always use the non-mutating alternatives in React state.
arr.map(item => item.id === id ? {...item, x: y} : item)
mutate the array in place. Always copy first.
Arrays of Objects: Update an Item's Field
Updating a field inside an array element requires both creating a new array (
) and creating a new object for the changed element (spread).

For deeply nested structures, manual spreading becomes error-prone. Immer is a library that lets you write mutative-looking code inside a producer function while producing a new immutable value under the hood.
function intercepts mutations on the draft and converts them into a structurally-shared immutable update. React receives a new top-level reference, so re-renders work correctly.
: unchanged parts of the state tree keep the same reference, while only the changed path gets new objects. React's memoisation tools (
) can check references cheaply to decide whether to skip re-renders.
The spread creates a new object, but the damage is already done — the original is mutated, previous snapshots that closed over it are affected.
Array.from or slice on an array of objects — does not deep-copy the items.
The copy is a new array, but each element is still the same object reference.
Using sort() or reverse() directly on state.
Lifting Immutable Helpers Out of Components
Pure functions that compute next state from previous state can be defined outside the component and reused — or unit-tested independently of React.
Both create shallow copies, but spread is more concise and widely used in modern React code.
A shopping cart requires adding items, updating quantities, and removing items — all immutably.
Never modify (mutate) existing state objects or arrays directly — React uses reference equality to detect changes.
For objects: use the spread operator (
For deeply nested state, spread at every level that changes, or use Immer to write cleaner update logic.
Immutability enables React's bail-out optimisations and structural sharing — unchanged subtrees keep their references and skip re-renders.
